title: Cancer-causing chemicals in your food and drinking water with Zach Bush

chunks.title: Introduction to Glyphosate and Its Impact
chunks.summary: The podcast introduces the issue of glyphosate, highlighting a startling statistic from 2007 that shows glyphosate contamination in 75% of air and rainwater samples in Mississippi. The discussion frames glyphosate not just as a chemical concern but as a reflection of humanity's relationship with nature and its consequences on health.

chunks.title: Historical Context of Glyphosate
chunks.summary: Zach Bush discusses the historical origins of glyphosate, tracing its roots back to its use as a chemical warfare agent. He highlights the transformation over the years from chemical warfare to agricultural application, and how its introduction was framed as a safe alternative to previously used toxic substances.

chunks.title: Environmental and Health Impacts

It details how glyphosate disrupts the shikimic pathway essential for amino acid production in plants, thereby affecting entire ecosystems, human health, and leading to a rise in chronic diseases and autoimmune disorders.

showing us the future it's showing us what we don't want to repeat but it's not showing us a path to the alternative way of being and so what happened with the organic industry is we stopped using chemicals but we started relying on other Technologies to do what the chemicals used to do which is namely to knock down your cover crops or your weeds so that you could have bare soil to plant plant in and what we tended to do is start to over plow instead of spray and in over plowing we we did the same damage and so if you look you know my aha moment came in 2018 when we were filming a documentary from the headwaters of Mississippi down to New Orleans to tell the story of the maps that I just mentioned and all that um if you want to see that short film 22 minutes it's Farm footprint um. us is is the org um and you can see those cancer maps overlaid with the spraying maps and all of that in a 22-minute film there but in our very first farm that we stopped at there happened to be a soil education uh group there and they were showing the difference between organic and conventional soils and their quality of of nutrient density and kind of Vitality and I was devastated to see that the organic soils were actually underperforming compared to Conventional chemical Agriculture and so I had set out to tell this film ra you know asked my company to put up all this money to make this film and then like day three I'm like oh my God we have the wrong premise here what the heck are we doing fortunately they had in that soil test you know about an hour later a demonstration of regenerative soil and its capacity to overcome the problem and what I saw was a a philosophy that started to go pro- future rather than anti- past and so or organic is a is is a symptom of we're anti-chemical but what the heck are we are we just you know more mechanized versions of trying to do the same thing what happened with regenerative agriculture is a recognition of of hundreds of years of indigenous cultures that had practiced soil management instead of crop production with the focus on the soil instead of the output it changed the relationship of the farmer or the homeowner to the end product and this this is kind of the key feeling of the planet now where instead of measuring how much we're extracting from the planet we start measuring how much we give to the planet and when Nature is given to when we start to provide for nature it's unbelievable the abundance that comes back to us and so this is kind of this rediscovery of the relationship of humanity and our soil systems you know globally is this regenerative movement and it's been popularized and really you know successfully you know demonstrated in biodynamic farming and um you know other forms of of Agriculture that date back hundreds of years and then some more recent ones that have come online new Korean farming and some of these other uh aspects of farming so regenerative agriculture in my you know world is kind of a bucket that we can throw a bunch of different farming practices in that have been soil Centric and have been focused on the relationship of the the producer towards producing soil and and healthy ecology rather than just measuring bushels of corn on the back side of that that equation so that's that was where we pivoted in 2018 to realizing that the movement needed to May regenerative and since I had been studying this stuff you know on the ground for all these years and didn't know the word regenerative agriculture at the time we we realized we were in to need to be part of a network of new organizations that were coming online to popularize the understanding of regener of Agriculture at the global scale and start to become storytellers to those Farmers that were already successfully showing this model at scale around the world in almost every ecosystem on the planet showing the success of soil management rather than Crop Production as a focus of their efforts and the incredible abundance that was coming out of that not just from a a a you know crop standpoint but actually from a soop sociology standpoint starting to resolve the the tragedy of no succession plans for most Farms on the ground because in the current chemical model there's such small revenue streams that are squeaked out of these things and you wake up every morning as a farmer to get on your tractor and kill something you're spr spring herbicides you're spraying pesticides you're in Hazmat GE mixing these chemicals together when kids see their parents engaged in this activity every day it's it's very uninspiring and then to see the stress of their parents as as bankruptcy comes closer and closer and their parents are trying not to lose the fifth generation farm and all of this and suicide rates are five times higher than any other industry in the world no wonder the average Farmer in the United States is now 70 years old without a secession plan because the younger Generations were told to go elsewhere go go do something else this is not your future and we were watching regenerative Farmers reverse that pattern where they were so en joy and amazement and spiritual enrichment for their relationship back to other land that their children were being inspired to rejoin that effort and to become part of that and children today are understanding the opportunity of a distributed economic system and so when these Farms are reimagining themselves is instead of being commodi these marketplac sell into some you know black box of corn sales instead let's develop a relationship with people that want healthy food on their plants and let's start growing things that can rebuild this bridge between farmer and eater and and that phenomenon of knowing the person that you're impacting is what resolves the suicide rates which resolves the secession planning problem and everything else and these these Elder Farmers that are making this transition in their 50s 60s and 70s don't know how to make that distributed leap but their grandchildren do their grandchildren know how to put up a website how to create a brand how to do social media how to do Crow funding so it's it's really cool we're watching this Young Generation pivot to their skill sets that they've learned in a technologically distributed vision of where the world is going and then chunks.title: Connecting Individuals to Nature and Food Systems
chunks.summary: Bush stresses the need for people to rediscover their connection to nature and food systems. This shift towards a deeper understanding of our role in the ecosystem can help unite efforts in addressing environmental and health crises.
chunks.transcript: Harris from White Oak pastures were just on Joe Rogan's podcast which I was happy to see I think the listeners of my podcast will be somewhat familiar with the term regenerative farming mostly in the context of animal Agriculture and raising cattle that are grass-fed and grass finished but beyond grass-feeding and grass finishing which is a term that can get muddied and greenwashed quote unquote these days this regenerative raising of animals for people that want to eat animal meat and organs is such an incredible thing I mean white oak pastures is situated right in the middle of Georgia but they're kind of this Beacon of Hope doing this and I've been to their Farm you know they have soil samples Zach and they have the neighbors's farm which is a Peanut Farm it's kind of light colored dirt and they have their Farm which is this dark colored dirt and they you know test the carbon in the two soils and so I completely Echo what you've seen is that um when you have regenerative soil it's a completely different thing than uh conventional soil or even organic soil so I'm so excited about this regenerative movement but man it's going to take a while to educate people about the value of this movement and you've have you been to Rome Ranch you've been to Rome Ranch in Texas right yeah yeah yeah I H sponsored their Earth Day with Farmers footprint um I think it was two years ago we were there and U did a big event there and we we were back on Earth Day this year with a smaller part of our team but yeah what they've done there to bring back these keystone species is so inspiring uh they they've brought back the bison to the to the you know Wilderness of Texas there and um we're now working on this globally with our nonprofit uh project biome is looking at how we return keystone species to river systems throughout the world to get our the reversal of our desertification that's happening globally how do we re Kickstart the natural generative cycle that the planet goes into every 14,000 years and so excitingly we're right at that thresh where the planet is ready to go back into its uh you know regreening of North Africa regreening of Saudi Arab reging of you know China and South Siberia onethird of the planet is ready to start on this regenerative Journey again but right now we have chemical agriculture kind of has the knee on the neck of of the planet and it can't take that next breath when we kill 97% of the aable soils on the planet which we've done over the last 50 years 97% of farmable land globally is depleted or severely depleted and its ability to respirate carbon that's why there's CO2 in the atmosphere and so this whole kind of politicized story of you know greenhouse gases and it's because we're burning fossil fuels is actually profoundly inaccurate in fact I think very dangerous to our future because it's it's externalizing the problem to a human behavior when in fact it's a biologic behavior of the planet itself that's leading to the accumulation of gas and this can be demonstrated very clearly by you know the data that came out of the pandemic in those first six months of 2020 we froze Transportation on this planet like no time history airline travel to near zero we had Global traffic to your work and everything else go to near zero as everybody's locked down in their homes China you know India Africa North America everybody locked down hard and yet the travel of CO2 and methane in the atmosphere actually accelerated that year not slowed down and so it is simply not the burning of fossil fuels that is is driving this pattern of accumulation of greenhouse gases it's the failure of the breath of the planet itself the lungs of the planet is the soil system of the planet and so as we have chemically denuded the lungs of the planet we can't take that next breath but the exciting thing to me is when we do take that next breath when Earth can breathe again the speed at which we're going to regreen may have never been seen in history of a four billion year Planet because we have more CO2 available to biology than perhaps it's ever existed before because we have mined all of that oil out the ground over the last 150 years all that oil being burned through fossil fuels and everything else has actually made the carbon available biology can't reach those deep fossil layers of oil but they can as it's brought to the surface and so interestingly for all of the damage we have done to the environment we may inadvertently have prepared the Earth for the most rapid and extreme Greening that this planet has ever done over the next thousand years and I really want young people to hear this we're not in climate crisis we're in climate metamorphosis we are about to see a planet that has never occurred before and it might take the extinction of humans to achieve that if we don't change our Behavior like we may need to just die out as we're quickly doing here because our biology is not independent of the planet and so a as we go extinct this planet is going to Skyrocket into its most explosive biodiversification and beautification that's probably ever done every Extinction has led to Radical by divers ation which increas increases the intelligence of species increases the the number of species in the rest remember the last Extinction took us from dinosaurs to mammals to birds to you know humans ultimately and and it took us from palms and ferns is the only floor of the planet to deciduous trees wild flowers and the like so the beauty of this planet today is the result direct result of an Extinction death of the Planet 55 million years ago and so the the imag ination of biology accelerates and compounds logarithmically when it's under an extinction level stress and so when we see this fear of viruses and all of this talk about this we need to remember that viruses are the expression of stressed nature and right now we have as human being the existential threat on the planet put the entire biologic system of Earth under its greatest stress in 55 million years and for that every species is reimagining what it could look like after this stress resolves and so it's exciting to know that right now on Earth and even probably in my bloodstream right now is all of the genetic possibilities of which could happen after this Extinction ends and the Earth has got more resources for to drive biology in the form of carbon than any time in history and it's important for us to remember just as we've demonized viruses which are the genetic potential of the future we've demonized carbon which is literally the gas tank in biology on Earth you cannot have a single leaf on a tree or a single earthworm or a single human without carbon as the fuel for that biological phenomenon to happen and so we keep demonizing in our politic politicalization of of our stories on climate we keep demonizing the very things that make life possible and it's this adversarial belief system and adversarial relationship to our nature that really needs to resolve and it goes all the way down to our deepest definitions if you look up the Oxford English Dictionary and then the word nature it says that nature defines everything on the planet including minerals rocks you know leaves the trees the animals everything except humans or anything humans have created and so somehow in our English language we decided to write humans and everything we've created right out of Nature and I think that is a symptom of this deep psychological wound that we carry around thinking that we did get rejected from nature some we got rejected from the garden that's in our myth of so many religions if we got rejected from nature then that must mean Nature's against us and so we have had this stance against nature for thousands of years and the rise and fall of Empires is driven out of the scarcity that happens when you don't think you belong to Nature when you don't think you have a mother when we reaccept nature as our mother as the source of life itself that made humans possible and as we accept that nature is literally speaking through our biology to a future event or at least to this present event but I think to this future event of what comes after our current iteration of humanity then we can resolve that fear Paradigm and when we lose the fear and also the guilt and shame we have to lose the guilt and shame that we the worst thing that ever happened on the planet we the cancer of the planet we the existential threat we need to lose the fear of Nature and also the guilt and shame of our relationship to that nature and start to realize that actually even in our worst mistake

chunks.title: Final Thoughts on Love and Healing
chunks.summary: The podcast ends on a hopeful note, emphasizing the transformational power of love in food and community. Bush shares a poignant story of how a compassionate relationship with animals can mitigate toxic effects, advocating for a future grounded in love and connection to nature.
